title: Parallel Goals

content.question: What two goals could share one action this week?
sop: List your current active goals across domains, business, health, relationships, learning
Look for one action that could genuinely serve two goals at once, not just in theory
Rate how many outputs a given action currently produces (see calculation, page 6)
Schedule the double-duty action deliberately, naming both goals it serves
Check monthly that the combined action is still serving both goals, not secretly favoring one
content.explainer: Most goals don't actually need separate time, they need separate attention, which is a much smaller ask. An action that's designed to serve two goals at once isn't cutting corners on either one; it's recognizing that the goals were never actually competing for the same resource, just for the same slot on your calendar.
questions: What two goals could share one action this week?
Which of your current goals are you pursuing in completely separate, non-overlapping actions that could actually be combined?
What's an action you're already committed to doing that's secretly capable of serving a second goal you haven't connected it to?
content.equation_example: Outputs per Action: a walk with a client equals 1 (relationship) plus 1 (exercise) equals 2 outputs from 1 action. A separate call and a separate workout equals 1 plus 1 equals 2 outputs from 2 actions. The combined version produces the same 2 outputs for half the action count. Higher outputs-per-action means more goals moving per unit of time spent; lower means your goals are quietly competing for the same hours.
content.closing_statement: One well-designed action can quietly serve two goals.

caption:
Principle Nº 235: Parallel Goals

Pursue complementary objectives together. Most goals don't actually need separate time, they need separate attention, which is a much smaller ask.

Ask yourself: What two goals could share one action this week?
